FFmpeg was started in 2000 by Fabrice Bellard, who also created QEMU and the fastest known algorithm for computing pi.
The "FF" in FFmpeg originally stood for "Fast Forward".
FFmpeg supports over 100 codecs and hundreds of container formats out of the box.
The H.264 codec is used by over 90% of all video on the internet.
Hardware encoding with NVENC can be up to 10x faster than software encoding, but produces slightly larger files at the same visual quality.
The MKV (Matroska) container format can hold virtually unlimited audio, video, and subtitle tracks in a single file.
A typical Blu-ray movie is around 25-50 GB, but can be re-encoded to under 5 GB with minimal visual loss using HEVC.
FFmpeg can process over 450 different pixel formats - far more than any human eye could distinguish.
The CRF (Constant Rate Factor) scale is logarithmic: CRF 18 produces roughly twice the file size of CRF 24 at the same resolution.
HEVC (H.265) can achieve the same quality as H.264 at roughly 50% of the file size, at the cost of slower encoding.
FFmpeg is used behind the scenes by YouTube, Netflix, VLC, and OBS - among many others.
The Opus audio codec, often used with WebM, was designed to handle everything from voice to music and beats most codecs at low bitrates.
Subtitles come in two flavors: text-based (SRT, ASS) which are tiny, and image-based (PGS, VobSub) which can be several MB.
Re-encoding audio from lossy to lossy (e.g., AAC → MP3) is called transcoding and always loses some quality - copying the stream avoids this.
The -preset flag doesn't change quality - it trades encoding speed for file size. Slower presets produce smaller files at the same quality.
